1: Direct: "Who is like the wise man? Who else knows the meaning of a matter?" — the rhetorical question that in the OT registers divine incomparability (cf. Micah 7:18 "who is a God like you?") here applied to wisdom itself. Paul identifies Christ as the answer: God has made Christ "to be for us wisdom" (1 Corinthians 1:30), and in him "are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge" (Colossians 2:3). The brightened face that wisdom produces echoes Moses' shining face and ultimately points to Christ, whose face shines with divine glory (2 Corinthians 3:18; Ecclesiastes 4:6).
2: The theme: keep the king's command, because of your oath before God — the obligation of loyalty sworn before the divine witness. Paul's instruction to submit to governing authorities (Romans 13:1) and Peter's "honor the king" (1 Peter 2:17) carry this Ecclesiastes wisdom into the NT context. Christ himself, the true King, commands loyalty; but the oath before God that undergirds all human authority is ultimately answered in the new covenant in his blood (Luke 22:20).
3: The revelation of God: do not hurry from the king's presence; do not persist in a bad cause — the wisdom of measured withdrawal from power. Christ's own navigating of kingly authority — "render to Caesar what is Caesar's" (Matthew 22:21) — reflects this Wisdom guidance, and his refusal to persist in a bad cause (he could have called legions of angels but did not — Matthew 26:53) is the ultimate expression of measured submission.
4: The theme: "the king's word is absolute" — no one says to him "what are you doing?" This characterization of royal authority resonates with the divine sovereignty language of Daniel and Job (Job 9:12; Ecclesiastes 33:13). Christ, the King of kings (Revelation 19:16), has absolute authority — "all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me" (Matthew 28:18) — yet he is not an arbitrary monarch but the righteous Judge who has himself submitted to every human authority unto death.
5: The theme: the one who obeys the king's command comes to no harm; the wise heart knows the proper time and method. The NT principle of discerning the right time — kairos — is rooted in this kind of wisdom: "making the most of every opportunity because the days are evil" (Ephesians 5:16). Christ operates with perfect discernment of time: "My hour has not yet come" (John 2:4), and when the hour arrives, he acts with complete authority.
6: The revelation of God: every purpose has its proper time and method, though trouble weighs heavily on the human being. The divine ordering of time — which no human can fully know — is made available to God's people through Christ, who is the same yesterday, today, and forever (Hebrews 13:8), and in whom the mystery of timing is not chaos but providential purpose (Ephesians 1:9–10: the mystery of the fullness of time to unite all things in Christ).
7: The theme: no one knows what is coming, and who can tell how it will come? The epistemic limit of mortal existence before the future is the condition Christ entered. He who "did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped" (Philippians 2:6) took on human unknowing — the one through whom all times are created submitted to not-knowing "that day or hour" (Mark 13:32) in his kenotic humility.
8: Direct: "No one has power over the life-breath to restrain it, and no one has power over the day of death." Paul quotes this register in 1 Corinthians 15: no one can escape death — "the last enemy" — by natural means. Christ's resurrection is the direct answer to this universal limit: he alone has "the keys of Death and Hades" (Revelation 1:18), the first and only one who has had power over the life-breath that all others must relinquish.
9: The revelation of God: one person dominating another to their own harm — the cycle of oppressive power. The divine observer noting this oppression is the God who judges between the oppressor and oppressed (Ps 82). Christ enters as the oppressed one (Isaiah 53:7) and his cross — the ultimate act of domination turned against him — becomes the point of liberation for all oppressed by sin and death (Galatians 5:1).
10: The theme: the wicked buried, soon forgotten in the city where they did wrong — the futility of an evil name. Against the evil name forgotten, Christ's good name (Ecclesiastes 7:1) is the name above every name (Philippians 2:9), exalted precisely because he was humiliated. Those who oppose his people — like the wicked here — will be remembered no more, while those in Christ receive his name and are known eternally.
11: The revelation of God: "when the sentence for a wrong is not carried out quickly, the human heart is emboldened to do evil" — the psychology of delayed judgment. The NT addresses this directly: "because sentence against an evil deed is not executed speedily, the human heart is fully set to do evil" (Ecclesiastes 8:11 stands behind Peter's warning that scoffers will say "Where is the promise of his coming?" — 2 Peter 3:3–4). Christ's return is the execution of the delayed sentence; the patience of God is his giving time for repentance (2 Peter 3:9).
12: Direct: "I know that it will go well with those who fear God, who stand in awe before him." This affirmation — that despite the sinner's long life and apparent prosperity, good will come to the God-fearer — is the OT covenant conviction. The NT places this on the far side of the cross: it will go well with those who are in Christ, because he has absorbed the fate of the wicked and secured the fate of the righteous (Romans 8:1, 28). The knowledge that it will go well is now anchored not in general principle but in a specific historical event.
13: Direct: "it will not go well with the wicked, and their days will not lengthen like a shadow, because they do not fear God." This is the covenantal declaration of judgment on the wicked. The NT does not soften but deepens it: the wicked who persist in wickedness apart from Christ face judgment (John 3:36; Revelation 20:15). The shadow-metaphor — days lengthening like a shadow before disappearing — matches the NT's picture of the brevity of earthly seeming-prosperity against eternal consequence.
14: The theme: the futility that occurs on earth — the righteous getting what the wicked deserve and vice versa — the observed injustice that is "futility." Christ experienced this directly: the righteous received the wages of the wicked (crucifixion as a criminal), and the wicked received release (Barabbas). His cross is the extreme case of this futility — and the resurrection is God's refusal to let it be the final word. The ultimate inversion of unjust inversions comes at the last judgment (Matthew 25:31–46).
15: The theme: Qohelet commends joy — eating, drinking, gladness accompanying work through all the days God has given under the sun. The NT receives this commendation: "whether you eat or drink, or whatever you do, do all to the glory of God" (1 Corinthians 10:31). Paul's instruction to "rejoice always" (Philippians 4:4) and the NT's persistent commendation of gladness in Christ is the fulfillment of Qohelet's limited joy — given full weight by the resurrection and the permanent day beyond the sun.
16: The revelation of God: the sleepless toil of those who labor day and night in searching — neither day nor night do they sleep — names the exhaustion of the human epistemic project. Christ's invitation "Come to me, all who are weary and heavy-laden, and I will give you rest" (Matthew 11:28) speaks directly to this sleeplessness. The unceasing labor of knowing is answered in the one who says "I am the truth" (John 14:6).
17: A shadow: "no one can discover what is done under the sun — no matter how much one labors, they cannot find it out." This is Ecclesiastes' fundamental epistemological limit. The NT's claim is that God has disclosed the mystery hidden for ages — in Christ (Colossians 1:26–27; Ephesians 3:3–6). The work of God that no one under the sun could discover has been revealed from above the sun; the mystery is now open in the one who came down from heaven (John 3:13).
